name: Superhero
description: Stories featuring extraordinary abilities or symbolic vigilante roles, emphasizing identity, moral responsibility, and conflict with major threats.

nodes.label: Superhero Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story about extraordinary individuals — people with powers, resources, or symbolic roles far beyond ordinary human capacity — who use those abilities to confront threats that ordinary people cannot, while grappling with who they are and what their power obligates them to do. The promise is not merely spectacle: it is the exploration of what it means to be capable of more than others and whether that capability is wielded with integrity.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as superhero fiction
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise — extraordinary protagonist, dual existence, proportional threats, and moral weight
typical_variants: Origin story (becoming the hero)
Vigilante/dark hero (moral ambiguity)
Team/ensemble hero (group dynamics)
Cosmic/mythic hero (civilizational stakes)
failure_modes: Hero has powers but no internal conflict about using them
Story is pure action spectacle with no identity or responsibility dimension
The extraordinary is treated as ordinary — no weight to what the hero can do
signals_in_text: Early establishment of extraordinary capability alongside personal vulnerability
Tension between the hero's power and the limits of what power can fix
Sense that this person's existence changes the moral landscape for everyone around them

nodes.label: Protagonist Must Possess Extraordinary Capacity
nodes.definition: The protagonist must operate at a level demonstrably beyond normal human capacity — whether through superhuman physical or mental abilities, advanced technology unavailable to others, vast resources used in a dedicated heroic role, or symbolic authority that grants them access and impact no ordinary person could achieve. This capacity must be story-central: it drives both what conflicts the hero can engage and what moral questions the story must answer.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Audience understands specifically what the protagonist can do that others cannot, and why that matters to the story's central conflict
typical_variants: Innate superpowers (flight, strength, energy manipulation)
Technology-based capacity (powered armor, gadgetry, weaponry)
Peak human ability with symbolic heroic identity (detective genius, martial arts master, tactical genius)
Magical or cosmic gift (divine empowerment, mystical artifacts, cosmic connection)
failure_modes: Protagonist's abilities are never clearly defined — audience can't track what they're capable of
Capacity is established but never challenged — no meaningful cost or limit
Extraordinary capacity is purely cosmetic and the story could proceed with an ordinary protagonist
signals_in_text: Early demonstration of capability that provokes awe or fear
Antagonist requires this level of capacity to be a meaningful threat
Other characters react to the protagonist's capacity as genuinely exceptional

nodes.label: Dual Identity Creates Structural Tension
nodes.definition: The hero must maintain two distinct identities — the heroic persona that engages the world's threats, and the civilian or personal identity that exists in ordinary life. These identities must create genuine tension: the hero persona demands action, secrecy, and sacrifice that continuously costs the civilian identity in relationships, opportunities, and psychological integration. The story is not merely about what the hero does in costume, but about the person behind the mask and whether those two halves can coexist.
entry_conditions: Extraordinary capacity established
exit_conditions: Audience understands both identities and feels the cost their coexistence imposes on the protagonist
typical_variants: Secret identity with mask and alias (civilian life must be protected)
Public identity with private emotional life (fame creates its own prison)
Reluctant hero who resists the heroic identity
Hero whose civilian identity has been destroyed by the role
failure_modes: Civilian identity is perfunctory — the hero is always the hero and the civilian life has no narrative weight
Dual identity exists but creates no meaningful conflict or cost
Secret is kept for plot convenience rather than character reasons
signals_in_text: Scenes in civilian life show what the hero role costs
Relationships strain or break because of what the hero cannot share or be
Protagonist must choose between heroic obligation and personal need, and either choice hurts

nodes.label: Threats Must Escalate to Match Heroic Capacity
nodes.definition: The antagonists and threats the hero faces must be proportional to the hero's extraordinary capacity — they must be threats that an ordinary person genuinely could not handle, requiring the hero's specific abilities to confront. As the hero's capacity is established, threats must escalate to match or exceed it, so the audience never loses belief in the danger. A hero who could defeat any threat effortlessly creates no story; a hero who is consistently pushed to the edge of their capacity creates tension.
entry_conditions: Protagonist's extraordinary capacity established
exit_conditions: Audience believes that each major threat genuinely requires the hero's capacity to engage — and that even that capacity may not be enough
typical_variants: Powered antagonist who matches or exceeds the hero's abilities
Threat at a scale that overwhelms the hero's individual capacity (city-wide, national, civilizational)
Antagonist who neutralizes the hero's specific advantage (kryptonite dynamic)
Threat that cannot be solved by power alone — requiring wisdom, restraint, or sacrifice
failure_modes: Threats are clearly below the hero's capacity — no tension
Threats escalate arbitrarily without logic connecting to the hero's power level
Hero defeats threats too easily and consistently for any scene to feel dangerous
signals_in_text: Hero is genuinely pushed to their limits or beyond
Antagonist demonstrates ability to hurt or stop the hero
Collateral damage or failure shows the stakes are real even when the hero wins

nodes.label: Power Creates Moral Obligation That Must Be Tested
nodes.definition: The story must engage with the fundamental moral question of the superhero genre: what does extraordinary power obligate a person to do, and what limits — if any — apply to how that power should be used? The hero's capacity is not morally neutral; it creates responsibility, and the story must test whether the hero lives up to that responsibility, abuses it, or is broken by the weight of it. Power without moral reckoning is spectacle; power with moral reckoning is superhero storytelling.
entry_conditions: Extraordinary capacity and threats established
exit_conditions: Story has meaningfully tested the hero's moral framework — whether through a direct moral dilemma, a failure that demands reckoning, or a cost that reveals what the hero truly values
typical_variants: 'With great power comes great responsibility' — obligation to act
The hero who uses power without oversight and must reckon with the consequences
The hero who holds back too much and pays the price for restraint
The hero who must choose between saving one person and saving many
failure_modes: Hero's moral framework is never challenged — always does the right thing with no cost
Moral questions are raised but resolved easily without genuine weight
Hero's power is treated as purely a combat resource with no ethical dimension
signals_in_text: Hero faces choices where there is no clearly right answer
Failure — someone dies or is harmed because of the hero's action or inaction — is treated with genuine gravity
Other characters challenge the hero's use of power, and those challenges have merit

nodes.label: Origin Story
nodes.definition: The story follows the protagonist's transformation from ordinary person (or extraordinary person without purpose) into the hero they will become. The origin is not merely about acquiring powers — it is about the moment when the hero accepts the identity, responsibility, and cost that comes with the role. The inciting event, the acquisition of capacity, the first use of power, and the first reckoning with what that power demands are all obligatory structural beats.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Protagonist begins in a pre-heroic state — without powers, without identity, or without commitment to the heroic role
exit_conditions: Protagonist has acquired their capacity, chosen the heroic identity, and confronted the first major cost of that choice
typical_variants: Accident-driven origin (radiation, experiment, lightning)
Tragedy-driven origin (loss that creates the mission)
Discovery origin (always had the power, now must choose what to do with it)
Legacy origin (inheriting a heroic role from another)
failure_modes: Origin is about power acquisition but skips the identity choice — the hero simply starts fighting without the internal transformation
Inciting tragedy is presented but its emotional weight is not carried through the story
Powers are explained but the moral framework they create is not established
signals_in_text: A before-and-after structure that makes the transformation visible
The inciting event is a wound the story never fully lets heal
The first use of heroic capacity is imperfect, costly, or morally complicated

nodes.label: Vigilante / Dark Hero
nodes.definition: The hero operates outside or in tension with sanctioned authority, and the story interrogates the moral legitimacy of that choice. The vigilante hero crosses or approaches lines — of legality, of violence, of certainty — that mainstream heroism does not, and the story must genuinely engage with what those crossings cost and mean. This is not simply a darker aesthetic; it is a pattern where moral ambiguity is load-bearing. The hero's methods are themselves part of what the story is about.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Hero operates without or against institutional sanction
Hero's methods include elements that are legally or morally transgressive
exit_conditions: Story has forced the hero to reckon with whether their transgressive methods are justified, and at what cost
typical_variants: Lethal vigilante who kills criminals (and must confront what that makes them)
Anti-hero whose methods parallel those of the villains they fight
Hero in conflict with law enforcement or government who views them as a threat
Hero who uses psychological terror or brutality as a tool
failure_modes: Vigilante methods are portrayed without moral scrutiny — the story endorses whatever the hero does
Dark tone is aesthetic rather than moral — the hero is gritty but their choices have no real weight
Vigilante status is never meaningfully challenged by other characters or events
signals_in_text: Other characters — allies, not just antagonists — challenge the hero's methods on legitimate grounds
Hero's methods have collateral costs that the story acknowledges
Hero's internal justification for crossing lines is shown to be unstable or self-serving

nodes.label: Team / Ensemble Superhero
nodes.definition: Multiple heroes with distinct capacities, identities, and moral frameworks must work together against threats that no individual hero could handle alone. The team pattern requires that individual stories remain present within the ensemble: each hero's dual identity, moral framework, and personal cost must remain meaningful, while the group dynamics — trust, friction, leadership, sacrifice — create a second layer of story above the individual threads.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply to each hero in the ensemble
Threat exceeds what any single hero can address
Team members have meaningfully different capacities, perspectives, or values that create friction and synergy
exit_conditions: Team has combined their capacities in a way that requires each member's distinct contribution — no single hero could have replaced the group — and the group dynamics have been tested
typical_variants: Reluctant team assembled by necessity
Established team with deep history and interpersonal fault lines
Team of outsiders or misfits who find belonging in each other
Rival hero factions who must cooperate despite ideological differences
failure_modes: Team members are interchangeable — any one of them could do what any other does
Individual character arcs collapse into pure team function — no one has a meaningful civilian or personal identity
Team conflict is trivial — resolved too quickly to have dramatic weight
signals_in_text: Scenes of genuine friction between heroes with different methods or values
A moment of breakdown or betrayal within the team that the story treats seriously
The team's victory requires a specific combination of individual contributions that couldn't be rearranged

nodes.label: Cosmic / Mythic Superhero
nodes.definition: The hero confronts threats at a civilizational, planetary, or cosmic scale — threats to entire worlds, timelines, or the fabric of existence — and the story operates in the register of myth, with archetypal forces and stakes beyond ordinary comprehension. The personal remains present — even a god-level hero must have a human emotional core — but the scope of conflict forces the hero into a symbolic role as a champion or defender of existence itself.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Threat operates at civilizational or cosmic scale — cannot be addressed by ordinary heroes
Hero's capacity is at or approaching god-tier — cosmic power, mythological significance, or world-historical importance
exit_conditions: Civilizational or cosmic stakes are resolved through the hero's capacity — and the personal cost to the hero is proportional to the scope of what was risked
typical_variants: Hero as demigod or divine champion facing ancient evil
Hero who must sacrifice fundamental identity or power to save existence
Heroes who operate as symbols — their defeat means more than tactical loss; it means the idea of hope is lost
Cosmic-scale conflict rooted in a deeply personal antagonist relationship
failure_modes: Scale is vast but the hero faces no personal cost — cosmic victory feels weightless
Human emotional core disappears — the hero becomes a force of nature with no inner life
Cosmic threat is introduced without establishing what it actually threatens in concrete terms
signals_in_text: What is at stake is defined in human terms — these are real people who will cease to exist, not abstract civilizations
Hero's personal identity is at risk alongside the world's survival
Antagonist has a comprehensible motivation even at cosmic scale

nodes.label: A Community Depends on the Hero
nodes.definition: The story must establish a city, community, or world that has a genuine relationship with the hero — one that depends on the hero's protection, that is shaped by the hero's presence, and whose fate is materially at stake in the hero's conflicts. This community is not mere backdrop; it is the moral justification for the hero's existence, the source of the hero's civilian identity, and the measure of what the hero succeeds or fails to protect. The city feels the hero's victories and failures.
entry_conditions: Hero's capacity and identity established
exit_conditions: Audience understands specifically what the hero is protecting and why it matters — both to the hero personally and in concrete terms of people and places
typical_variants: Iconic city with a distinct character that the hero is identified with (Gotham, Metropolis, New York)
Small community or neighborhood that humanizes the hero's scale
Global or cosmic community that the hero defends at the cost of personal connection
The hero's community has complicated feelings about the hero — gratitude and fear coexist
failure_modes: Community is purely abstract — 'protecting innocent people' with no specific faces or stakes
Hero's actions destroy the community they're protecting with no reckoning
Community is present but has no relationship with the hero — they might as well not be there
signals_in_text: Specific people in the community who appear across scenes and have stakes in the hero's success
The hero's civilian identity exists within this community and its relationships
The cost of the hero's battles is visible in damage, fear, or loss within the community

nodes.label: Power Has Rules and Defined Limits
nodes.definition: The hero's extraordinary capacity must operate according to consistent rules that define both what the hero can do and what they cannot. These limits are not obstacles to be overcome — they are the structural conditions that make the hero's struggle meaningful. A hero who can do anything faces nothing. The limits may be physical (energy reserves, specific vulnerabilities), tactical (can stop a bullet but not a bad decision), or moral (chooses not to use full capacity), but they must be present and enforced.
entry_conditions: Hero's capacity established
exit_conditions: Audience can reliably predict what the hero's powers will and will not accomplish in a given situation
typical_variants: Specific elemental weakness (kryptonite, cold, magic)
Resource-based limit (power source must recharge, suit can be damaged)
Scope limit (vast power in one domain, helpless in another)
Self-imposed limit (hero refuses to kill, refuses to invade privacy, etc.)
failure_modes: Hero's limits are established but ignored when the plot requires — the power does whatever is needed
Limits exist only to be overcome at the climax, serving as a fake obstacle
Hero is effectively omnipotent within the story's conflict — threats cannot touch them
signals_in_text: Hero explicitly cannot solve a problem their powers would seem to handle
Villain exploits a known weakness in a way the hero cannot simply overcome by trying harder
The hero's limits create genuine narrative problems that require non-power solutions

nodes.label: Villain Ecosystem Mirrors and Challenges the Hero
nodes.definition: The antagonists in a superhero story are not random obstacles — they exist in deliberate relationship to the hero. The villain ecosystem must include antagonists who mirror the hero (similar capacity, different moral framework), invert the hero (the dark outcome of the same origin or power), challenge the hero's method (enemies who make the hero's approach seem inadequate or wrong), or simply scale to require the hero's specific capacity to defeat. The villain's existence should illuminate something essential about the hero.
entry_conditions: Hero's capacity, identity, and moral framework established
exit_conditions: Primary antagonist has a comprehensible motivation and a specific relationship to the hero that goes beyond mere opposition — defeating the villain will require the hero to confront something about themselves
typical_variants: Mirror villain (same powers, opposite values — the dark hero)
Inverted origin (what the hero could have become with different choices)
Ideological challenger (villain whose worldview has internal logic the hero cannot simply dismiss)
Personal villain (someone whose grievance against the hero is legitimate even if their methods are not)
failure_modes: Villain exists only as a power-level obstacle with no thematic relationship to the hero
Villain's motivation is cartoonish or incomprehensible — no moral weight to their opposition
Villain is defeated by the hero's power alone without engaging the thematic relationship between them
signals_in_text: Hero and villain scenes have dialogue or subtext that reveals something about both of them
Understanding the villain's perspective creates at least partial sympathy or recognition
The hero's victory over the villain requires engaging with the villain's challenge, not just their power

nodes.label: Power Demonstration Scene
nodes.definition: An early scene — ideally in the first act — that establishes the hero's extraordinary capacity in vivid, concrete action. The audience must see what the hero can do in a way that creates genuine awe, establishes the scale of their ability, and demonstrates the rules that govern how that ability works. This scene sets the benchmark against which all subsequent threats will be measured — if the audience does not understand what the hero is capable of, they cannot feel the danger when threats challenge that capacity.
entry_conditions: Hero's capacity has been introduced conceptually
exit_conditions: Audience has seen the hero's capacity in action and understands both its impressive range and at least one meaningful limit
typical_variants: Rescue scene that demonstrates both power and the hero's protective instinct
Training or test sequence that establishes the rules of the power system
First confrontation with a minor threat that shows the hero's capabilities without full risk
Accidental or uncontrolled power use that demonstrates scope while creating a problem
failure_modes: Power is described but not shown — told not demonstrated
Demonstration shows only upside with no hint of limits or cost
Scene exists to impress rather than to establish narrative rules
signals_in_text: Specific abilities are shown with enough detail that the audience internalizes the rules
Reaction of bystanders or other characters registers the genuinely extraordinary nature of what they've seen
At least one element of the demonstration establishes a limit, vulnerability, or cost

nodes.label: Identity Crisis Scene
nodes.definition: A scene — typically in the second act but potentially recurring throughout — where the tension between the heroic identity and the civilian identity reaches a breaking point. The hero is forced to confront what the role is costing them, whether the identity is sustainable, and who they actually are beneath the mask. This is not merely a sad scene: it is a structural beat where the hero's dual existence becomes existentially threatening, and the audience feels the full weight of what the hero's capacity has taken from them.
entry_conditions: Dual identity established
Costs of heroic role have been accumulating through the story
exit_conditions: Hero has directly confronted the question of whether they can continue — and chosen, actively, to recommit to the heroic identity or to question it in a way the story carries forward
typical_variants: Hero loses someone because the civilian identity couldn't protect them
Hero's civilian identity is discovered, threatening everyone they love
Hero reaches the limit of what they can endure and considers abandoning the role
Hero faces evidence that their heroic actions have caused the harm they set out to prevent
failure_modes: Crisis is raised and resolved in the same scene with no lasting impact
Crisis is about losing powers rather than losing the self — external rather than internal
Hero commits to continuing without genuine reckoning — the choice costs nothing
signals_in_text: Hero is shown without the mask in a context that emphasizes their civilian vulnerability
The specific cost is personal and named — a relationship, an opportunity, a part of themselves they cannot recover
Other characters see the hero as human — with need, fear, and limits — not just as a symbol

nodes.label: Climactic Battle Scene
nodes.definition: The final confrontation with the primary threat — the battle that resolves the story's central conflict and tests everything the hero has been through. The climactic battle must require more than the hero's raw capacity: it must require the moral framework established through the story, the specific lesson of the identity crisis, and the relationship with the villain ecosystem. The hero wins (or loses) not purely through power, but through who they have chosen to be. Physical spectacle serves the emotional and moral resolution — not the reverse.
entry_conditions: All prior escalation has reached its peak
Hero's capacity and moral framework have both been tested
Stakes are clear — the outcome of this battle has defined consequences for the community and the hero's identity
exit_conditions: Primary threat is resolved in a way that requires the hero's specific capacity and their specific moral choice — power alone was insufficient, and character determined the outcome
typical_variants: Hero must sacrifice something fundamental to win — power, relationship, or life
Hero defeats the villain not by overpowering them but by refusing to become them
Hero's victory is pyrrhic — the threat is stopped but at a cost the story does not minimize
Hero loses the battle but wins by inspiring others — the victory is transferred
failure_modes: Hero wins by simply overpowering the villain — moral dimension is irrelevant to the outcome
Climax resolves through external intervention rather than the hero's own agency and choice
Battle spectacle crowds out the emotional resolution — the fight is impressive but meaningless
signals_in_text: The moment of victory is inseparable from a moral choice the hero makes
Villain's defeat connects to the thematic relationship between them — the villain is undone by what the hero represents
The aftermath of battle shows the cost clearly — victory is not clean

nodes.label: Heroic Grandeur Mixed with Personal Vulnerability
nodes.definition: The superhero genre requires a tone that holds two registers simultaneously: heroic grandeur — the sense that these individuals are extraordinary, that their conflicts have epic significance, and that the stakes are real and large — and personal vulnerability — the sense that beneath the symbol is a person who can be hurt, who doubts, who loses things they cannot get back. Neither register alone is sufficient. Pure grandeur produces hollow spectacle; pure vulnerability produces a character study that fails the genre promise. The tonal achievement is keeping both present at once.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Story consistently moves between the epic and the intimate — the same events register as both historically significant and personally devastating
typical_variants: Operatic grandeur with intimate emotional undercurrent
Gritty realism that never loses sight of the symbolic significance
Mythic scale delivered through street-level human detail
Tragedy and triumph held in genuine tension
failure_modes: Tone collapses into pure spectacle — impressive but emotionally hollow
Tone collapses into pure pathos — emotionally effective but the heroic dimension disappears
Grandeur and vulnerability are present but in separate, non-integrated sequences
signals_in_text: The same scene or speech carries both the weight of historic significance and specific personal cost
Action sequences are lit and scored for grandeur but framed through characters who can be hurt
Moments of triumph are immediately shadowed by what winning cost

nodes.label: Invincible Hero with No Stakes
nodes.definition: The superhero genre fails when the hero is effectively invincible — capable of defeating any threat without genuine risk, without meaningful limits, and without personal cost. This is the 'Superman problem' in its pathological form: the hero is so powerful that no conflict generates authentic tension, and victory is only a matter of time. The problem is not the hero's power level per se — it is the failure to ensure that power is matched by proportional threats, defined limits, and genuine moral and personal stakes that power alone cannot resolve. Invincibility eliminates the story.
entry_conditions: Hero engages threats in the story
exit_conditions: Every significant conflict genuinely threatens the hero — physically, morally, personally, or in terms of the community they protect
typical_variants: Physical invincibility (cannot be meaningfully harmed)
Moral invincibility (never faces a genuine dilemma — always knows the right answer)
Emotional invincibility (losses don't accumulate — hero bounces back from everything)
Strategic invincibility (hero always has a plan that works)
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience stops worrying about outcomes
Villain's threat is dispelled too quickly to register
Hero's choices have no visible cost — each situation resolves without lasting consequence

edges.label: Promise requires extraordinary protagonist capacity
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The scale of what the hero can do determines the scale of threats that create meaningful conflict
edges.effects_on_character: The nature and limits of the hero's capacity shape every decision they make and every moral question they face
common_alternatives: Innate superpower vs. technology-based capacity vs. peak-human symbolic hero
anti_patterns: Protagonist is extraordinary in backstory but ordinary in the story's actual conflict — the capacity is not narrative-functional

edges.label: Promise requires dual identity tension
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The civilian identity is a vulnerability that antagonists can exploit and a value that motivates the hero's restraint
edges.effects_on_character: The hero's psychological integrity depends on how they navigate the two identities — fracture here is existential
common_alternatives: Secret identity vs. public identity vs. hero who has sacrificed civilian life entirely
anti_patterns: Dual identity exists as a plot device (disguise) rather than a character truth (internal division)

edges.label: Promise requires threats proportional to heroic capacity
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Proportional threats ensure that the hero's victories are earned and their failures are credible
edges.effects_on_character: Threats that match the hero's capacity force genuine exertion — moral, physical, and personal
common_alternatives: Single overwhelming villain vs. escalating threat ecosystem vs. systemic threat that resists individual heroism
anti_patterns: Hero defeats disproportionately weak threats that could be handled by ordinary people — extraordinary capacity is wasted

edges.label: Promise requires moral responsibility as a structural element
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Moral responsibility raises every conflict above the level of pure combat — the question is never only 'can the hero win' but 'should they, and at what cost'
edges.effects_on_character: Hero's choices are morally consequential — their capacity makes them responsible for outcomes they didn't directly cause
common_alternatives: Obligation frame (hero must act) vs. choice frame (hero chooses to act) vs. cost frame (hero pays for acting)
anti_patterns: Hero's power is treated as morally neutral — a tool with no ethical dimension

edges.label: Promise requires heroic grandeur mixed with personal vulnerability
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone ensures that epic-scale events register as personally meaningful — and personal events register as having epic significance
edges.effects_on_character: Hero is simultaneously symbol and person — the tone must hold both without collapsing into either
common_alternatives: Operatic tone vs. gritty realist tone vs. mythic tone — all valid if they maintain the dual register
anti_patterns: Pure spectacle tone (grandeur without vulnerability) or pure character-study tone (vulnerability without grandeur)

edges.label: Capacity must be acquired and identity chosen — origin subtype
preconditions: Protagonist begins without heroic capacity or without commitment to heroic identity
edges.effects_on_stakes: Origin structure means the audience witnesses the full cost of what the hero becomes — nothing is given for free
edges.effects_on_character: The wound that creates the hero is also what the hero carries forever — origin is not backstory, it is permanent character condition
common_alternatives: Vigilante pattern
Team pattern
Cosmic pattern
anti_patterns: Origin is about power acquisition without the identity choice — hero simply starts fighting with new abilities

edges.label: Moral responsibility tested through transgressive methods — vigilante subtype
preconditions: Hero's moral framework includes willingness to operate outside sanctioned authority
Hero's methods cross lines that raise legitimate questions
edges.effects_on_stakes: The vigilante's moral authority is itself at stake — the hero risks becoming what they fight
edges.effects_on_character: Hero must continually justify to themselves and others why their transgressive methods are not simply violence with a costume
common_alternatives: Origin pattern
Team pattern
Cosmic pattern
anti_patterns: Vigilante methods are glorified without scrutiny — moral transgression is treated as heroism without question

edges.label: Multiple dual identities create ensemble complexity — team subtype
preconditions: Multiple heroes each with their own dual identities and moral frameworks
Threat exceeds individual capacity
edges.effects_on_stakes: Team failure means more than one hero's defeat — it represents the collapse of a collective identity and shared mission
edges.effects_on_character: Each hero's individual arc must remain present within the ensemble — the team does not erase the individual
common_alternatives: Origin pattern
Vigilante pattern
Cosmic pattern
anti_patterns: Team members lose individual identity — become defined by their power set rather than their personhood

edges.label: Threats at civilizational or cosmic scale — cosmic subtype
preconditions: Threat operates at scale beyond individual or city-level
Hero's capacity has been elevated to match cosmic stakes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Cosmic scale raises everything — but must remain grounded in what actual beings will lose, not just abstract civilizations
edges.effects_on_character: Hero at cosmic scale risks losing the personal dimension — must be anchored to specific people and specific choices that feel human
common_alternatives: Origin pattern
Vigilante pattern
Team pattern
anti_patterns: Cosmic scale without human grounding — the story operates at a level of abstraction where nothing is felt

edges.label: Origin establishes the community the hero is born into and will protect
preconditions: Origin subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The inciting tragedy that creates the hero is often rooted in this community — protecting it is personal
edges.effects_on_character: Hero's civilian identity exists within this community — the city is the texture of the person behind the mask
common_alternatives: Hero's community is global or abstract
Hero protects a community they were never part of and must earn belonging in
anti_patterns: Community is mentioned as what the hero protects but never concretized — no specific faces or stakes

edges.label: Vigilante pattern requires defined limits that test whether the hero will cross them
preconditions: Vigilante subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The hero's limits — especially self-imposed ones — are the moral battleground of the vigilante story
edges.effects_on_character: Whether the hero holds to their limits under pressure defines who they are — and breaking them has consequences the story must honor
common_alternatives: No-kill rule under pressure
Refusing to invade privacy despite tactical advantage
Refusing to use maximum force even when it would resolve the conflict
anti_patterns: Hero's stated limits are abandoned without meaningful reckoning — the limit was hollow from the start

edges.label: Team pattern requires villain ecosystem that matches ensemble capacity
preconditions: Team subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Villain ecosystem must be as diverse and coordinated as the hero team — requiring each hero's specific contribution to defeat
edges.effects_on_character: Different villains target different heroes' specific weaknesses or mirror different heroes' specific identities
common_alternatives: Single massive villain who challenges all heroes at once
Villain faction whose members parallel the hero team
Ideological villain whose challenge is directed at the team as an institution rather than individual members
anti_patterns: One villain that any single hero could defeat is given to a team — the ensemble is irrelevant to the threat

edges.label: Cosmic scale makes power limits structurally essential — something must be able to threaten a god-tier hero
preconditions: Cosmic subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without limits at cosmic scale, the conflict is inert — if nothing can threaten a cosmic hero, there is no story
edges.effects_on_character: Cosmic-scale limits are often moral or personal rather than physical — the hero can destroy but cannot save; can fight but cannot protect everyone
common_alternatives: Specific cosmic vulnerability (equivalent substance or energy type)
Moral limit that becomes a liability at cosmic scale
Scale limit — cosmic power cannot solve intimate personal failure
anti_patterns: Cosmic-level hero has no defined limits — the story becomes an exercise in inventing arbitrary obstacles

edges.label: The community provides the context and stakes for the power demonstration scene
preconditions: Community established as what the hero protects
edges.effects_on_stakes: Power demonstration is not an abstraction — it is shown in terms of what the hero protects and what happens when they act
edges.effects_on_character: The hero's first demonstration of capacity reveals their relationship to the community — protector, stranger, or something more complicated
common_alternatives: Public rescue with civilian witnesses
Private training or test with no community present
Demonstration in response to a specific threat to a specific person in the community
anti_patterns: Power is demonstrated in a vacuum with no relationship to the community the hero will protect

edges.label: Encountering genuine limits triggers the identity crisis — what is the hero without the power to fix everything?
preconditions: Hero's limits have been tested — something the hero cannot fix with their capacity has occurred
edges.effects_on_stakes: The identity crisis is precipitated by the discovery that power is not enough — the hero's capacity cannot deliver what the heroic identity promises
edges.effects_on_character: Limits reveal what the hero has been avoiding: that the role costs more than power can pay, and that who they are without power matters as much as what they can do with it
common_alternatives: Limit is physical (loss of power) and triggers crisis about civilian self
Limit is moral (won't use full power) and triggers crisis about whether restraint is worth the cost
Limit is relational (power cannot protect loved ones from all harm) and triggers crisis about the civilian identity's sustainability
anti_patterns: Hero encounters limits but does not experience a genuine identity crisis — the limits bounce off without internal consequence

edges.label: Villain ecosystem converges on climactic battle that requires engaging the thematic relationship
preconditions: Villain's thematic relationship to the hero has been established
Final confrontation is with the primary threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: The climactic battle's outcome depends not just on power but on the hero engaging the villain's specific challenge — what the villain represents about the hero must be faced
edges.effects_on_character: Defeating the villain requires the hero to resolve the thematic tension the villain embodies — it is a moral and existential victory, not just a physical one
common_alternatives: Hero defeats villain by refusing to become them (the villain's method is their undoing)
Hero wins by understanding the villain's motivation and finding a solution the villain couldn't anticipate
Hero loses the battle but wins by what they represent — the idea survives even if the person doesn't
anti_patterns: Climactic battle is a power contest with no moral or thematic dimension — whoever hits harder wins

edges.label: Identity crisis resolved through recommitment that enables climactic action
preconditions: Identity crisis has been experienced — hero has genuinely questioned whether to continue
edges.effects_on_stakes: The climactic battle is fought by a hero who has consciously chosen to be there — not through circumstance but through recommitment after genuine doubt
edges.effects_on_character: The hero who fights the climax has paid the cost of the identity crisis — and that cost is what makes their choice to fight heroic rather than obligatory
common_alternatives: Crisis resolved just before battle begins — hero arrives transformed
Crisis is resolved during battle — the turning point is the moment of recommitment under maximum pressure
Crisis is not resolved — hero fights without certainty and the battle forces the resolution
anti_patterns: Identity crisis disappears between scenes with no resolution — the hero simply shows up for the climax without having answered the question the crisis posed

edges.label: Disproportionately weak threats produce the invincible hero anti-pattern
preconditions: Threats consistently fall below the hero's capacity — hero is never genuinely challenged
edges.effects_on_stakes: Invincibility eliminates tension — the outcome of every conflict is predetermined
edges.effects_on_character: A hero who cannot be challenged cannot grow, cannot fail, and cannot choose — the character becomes a mechanism rather than a person
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Villain ecosystem calibrates and specializes the threat to the hero's specific capacity
preconditions: Hero's specific capacity and limits are established
Villain is designed in thematic and tactical relationship to those specifics
edges.effects_on_stakes: Specialized threats ensure the hero cannot simply apply maximum power — the villain knows how to make the hero's specific advantages irrelevant or costly
edges.effects_on_character: A villain calibrated to the hero's specific weakness forces the hero to develop new dimensions — they cannot just be who they already are
common_alternatives: Villain exploits physical weakness (kryptonite dynamic)
Villain exploits moral limit (forces the hero to choose between killing and letting others die)
Villain exploits the civilian identity (threatens those the hero loves)
Villain exploits the community relationship (turns the city against the hero)
anti_patterns: Villain is a generic threat with no specific relationship to the hero's capacity — any hero could fight this villain
